summary: I live in the north part of Seattle, and it's where I connect with my community, especially through the Seattle Chapter of the Asian Pacific American Labor Alliance. Seattle has been my home for many years, and it's where I work at the Post Office. The city provides a backdrop for much of my social and political involvement.

I've been a member of the Asian Pacific American Labor Alliance, or APALA, since around 1998, shortly after it was formed. Being part of APALA connects me to the Asian Pacific American community and labor activism in Seattle. It’s one of my main group affiliations alongside my union work, and I've stayed involved for over 20 years.

I've worked at the United States Postal Service since 1986, and currently I’m a Lead Clerk. My job involves managing the daily operations at the post office, including helping customers, sorting mail, and overseeing staff breaks. Working at USPS has been a long-term career for me, and it’s where I’ve taken on leadership roles within the workplace.

I’ve been a member of the American Postal Workers Union since I started working at the post office in 1986. Over the years, I’ve served in several union offices, including vice president of my local, steward, assistant craft director, legislative director, and sergeant at arms. The union is important to me because it supports workers’ rights and political activities related to our jobs.

The COVID-19 pandemic changed how we worked at the post office significantly. Starting in early 2020, we had to wear masks, require customers to do the same, and maintain social distancing with floor markers. Plexiglass shields were installed for protection, and many traditions like holiday meals were canceled to keep everyone safe. Despite challenges like cold air from open doors, we adapted to keep serving the public as essential workers.

I live and work in Washington state, specifically in Seattle, and during the COVID-19 pandemic, the governor designated us postal workers as essential under the stay home stay healthy order. This meant we had to continue working through the pandemic with new safety protocols. Washington’s policies shaped how we adapted our workplace to protect ourselves and the public.
cues: governor’s stay home stay healthy order
